one_line_takeaway: A template for applying the traditional OWASP Risk Rating Methodology to LLM systems, pairing a stakeholder-mapped threat matrix over the OWASP Top 10 for LLM with a three-step scenario/dependency/impact scoring process, demonstrated (not validated) on a hypothetical university chatbot.
key_finding: On the hypothetical University Virtual Assistant, the method assigns Prompt Injection a HIGH risk rating (likelihood 6.75 = High, driven by automated discovery tools, easy exploitation, and public knowledge of the vulnerability; final impact 4.5 = Medium) and Training Data Poisoning a MEDIUM rating (likelihood 4.25 = Medium, lowered by difficult discovery/exploit despite anonymous attackers; final impact 5.5 = Medium). The authors argue this lets a practitioner justify prioritizing prompt-injection mitigation over data-poisoning mitigation. A methodological point they emphasize: impact factors should be held to worst-case and kept independent of defensive improvements (hardening lowers likelihood, not impact).
core_contribution: A proposal/methodology paper (not an empirical study) that adapts the OWASP Risk Rating Methodology -- originally designed for traditional IT/web systems -- to LLM-based systems. The authors' own contribution is threefold: (1) a three-step semi-quantitative risk-analysis process consisting of Scenario Analysis (estimating threat-agent factors: skill, motive, opportunity, size), Dependency Mapping (aligning affected system components with vulnerability factors: ease of discovery, ease of exploit, awareness, intrusion detection), and Impact Analysis (technical + business impact), plugged into the OWASP Risk = Likelihood x Impact formula; (2) a generic 'threat matrix' (Table I) that takes the 10 categories from OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v1.1.0 and, for each, documents causes, consequences, static/dynamic controls, whether it is a 'traditional' cyber risk, and which of three stakeholder groups it affects; and (3) a worked demonstration on a hypothetical 'University Virtual Assistant' use case that fills in the actual OWASP scores. The novel framing is stakeholder-centric: mapping LLM risks to (a) fine-tuning developers, (b) third-party-API integration developers, and (c) end users.
threat_model: The paper does not introduce a new attack; it is a defender-side risk-assessment framework covering the full OWASP Top 10 for LLM adversary surface: prompt injection (direct 'jailbreaking' and indirect via external content), insecure output handling, training-data poisoning, model denial of service, supply-chain vulnerabilities, sensitive information disclosure, insecure plugin design, excessive agency, overreliance/hallucination, and model theft. Threat agents are parameterized per-scenario (e.g., an authenticated student or an attacker who bypassed auth for prompt injection; an anonymous Internet user uploading a poisoned dataset to HuggingFace for data poisoning). The intended users of the framework are security practitioners, developers, and decision-makers who need to prioritize mitigation and allocate resources for LLM-integrated systems.
limitations: Entirely proposal-stage: no empirical validation, no real system, no user study, and the single use case is admittedly hypothetical with author-assigned scores, so the framework's outputs are only as objective as the subjective 0-9 ratings a practitioner supplies (the authors themselves classify it as 'semi-quantitative'). Coverage is fully bounded by the OWASP Top 10 v1.1.0 snapshot and inherits its staleness. Only 2 of 10 risks are worked through. The threat matrix (Table I) leaves probability/impact/rating cells blank by design (it is a template). The authors flag that real-world case studies are needed future work to test effectiveness. Also note the OWASP Top 10 ordering/labels in Table I are inconsistent with the section text (e.g., LLM02 is labeled 'Insecure Plugin Design' in Table I but 'Insecure Output Handling' in the body), reflecting the living-document churn.

datasets_used: No real datasets or models are used or trained; the paper is a methodology proposal.

The only evaluation vehicle is a hypothetical 'University Virtual Assistant' use case (a chatbot fictionally built by fine-tuning an open-source pre-trained LLM on university course/administrative data with a two-tier knowledge base).
Referenced (not used) model families cited as stakeholder examples: Llama-2, Mistral (fine-tuning developers); GPT-3.5/GPT-4 via OpenAI API (integration developers).
quantitative_results: Prompt Injection (use case): Likelihood score 6.75 -> High; Technical Impact 3, Business Impact 6, Final Impact 4.5 -> Medium; Risk Severity HIGH.

Prompt Injection factor scores: skill 6, motive 4, opportunity 7, size 6; ease of discovery 9, ease of exploit 5, awareness 9, intrusion detection 8; loss of confidentiality 5, loss of accountability 7; financial damage 7, reputation 5, non-compliance 5, privacy violation 7.
Training Data Poisoning (use case): Likelihood score 4.25 -> Medium; Technical Impact 4, Business Impact 7, Final Impact 5.5 -> Medium; Risk Severity MEDIUM.

Training Data Poisoning factor scores: skill 6, motive 4, opportunity 0, size 9; ease of discovery 3, ease of exploit 3, awareness 1, intrusion detection 8; loss of integrity 7, loss of accountability 9; financial damage 7, reputation 9, non-compliance 5, privacy violation 7.
OWASP scoring scale: each factor 0-9; Risk = Likelihood x Impact; framework spans all 10 OWASP Top 10 for LLM v1.1.0 categories mapped to 3 stakeholder groups.
baselines_compared: No quantitative baseline comparison (no experiments). Related frameworks are contrasted qualitatively as motivation/related work, NOT as the paper's contribution: OWASP Top 10 for LLM and MITRE ATLAS (criticized for lacking stakeholder-specific severity/risk-assessment); Cui et al. 4-module LLM risk taxonomy with benchmarking; Mauri & Damiani STRIDE-AI (FMEA-based); Wilhjelm & Younis STRIDE/DFD for ML-based systems using Microsoft SDL AI/ML Bug Bar; Q. Zou et al. ML-SSA graph-based analysis; Berryville Institute's 78 generic ML risks / top-10; Kapoor & Bommasani marginal-risk framework for open foundation models.
Traditional risk frameworks named as the borrowed basis: OWASP Risk Rating Methodology (chosen), NIST SP 800-30, Canadian Centre for Cyber Security Harmonized TRA, ENISA RMF, NIST RMF, ISO 27001.
